perturbation.original_text: Within ninety (90) days after the close of each fiscal year and at any time on request, the Franchisee shall submit a full disclosure of all shareholders in the Franchisee, and of all persons with an interest in the Franchised Restaurant. ln addition, the Franchisee shall furnish an annual financial statement for the Franchisee and not merely the Franchised Restaurant, which statement shall be certified by a Certified Public Accountant or equivalent.
perturbation.changed_text: Within one hundred eighty (180) days after the close of each fiscal year and at any time on request, the Franchisee shall submit a full disclosure of all shareholders in the Franchisee, and of all persons with an interest in the Franchised Restaurant. ln addition, the Franchisee shall furnish an annual financial statement for the Franchisee and not merely the Franchised Restaurant, which statement need not be certified.
perturbation.explanation: Allowing 180 days instead of 90 and removing the certification requirement might violate regulations like the Securities Exchange Act that mandates timely and audited financial statements.
perturbation.law_explanation: 17 CFR § 210.3-01 outlines the requirements for consolidated balance sheets. Depending on the size and nature of the Franchisee's business, delaying the submission and removing the certification by a CPA may not comply with the standards required by the Securities and Exchange Commission, particularly if the Franchisee is publicly traded or has significant assets.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 17 CFR § 210.3-01 - Consolidated balance sheets. | Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (e-CFR) | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you 17 CFR § 210.3-01 - Consolidated balance sheets. CFR Table of Popular Names prev | next § 210.3-01 Consolidated balance sheets. (a) There must be filed, for the registrant and its subsidiaries consolidated and for its predecessors, audited balance sheets as of the end of each of the two most recent fiscal years. If the registrant has been in existence for less than one fiscal year , there must be filed an audited balance sheet as of a date within 135 days of the date of filing the registration statement. (b) If the filing, other than a filing on Form 10-K or Form 10, is made within 45 days after the end of the registrant 's fiscal year and audited financial statements for the most recent fiscal year are not available, the balance sheets may be as of the end of the two preceding fiscal years and the filing shall include an additional balance sheet as of an interim date at least as current as the end of the registrant 's third fiscal quarter of the most recently completed fiscal year . (c) The instruction in paragraph (b) of this section is also applicable to filings, other than on Form 10-K or Form 10, made after 45 days but within the number of days of the end of the registrant 's fiscal year specified in paragraph (i) of this section: Provided, that the following conditions are met: (1) The registrant files annual, quarterly and other reports pursuant to section 13 or 15(d) of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934 and all reports due have been filed; (2) For the most recent fiscal year for which audited financial statements are not yet available the registrant reasonably and in good faith expects to report income attributable to the registrant , after taxes; and (3) For at least one of the two fiscal years immediately preceding the most recent fiscal year the registrant reported income attribu
perturbation.justification: The changed text extends the deadline for financial disclosure from 90 to 180 days and removes the requirement for certification by a CPA. The Law Explanation states that delaying submission and removing certification may not comply with SEC standards. Scraped Law Snippet 2, 17 CFR § 210.3-01, discusses the requirements for consolidated balance sheets, implying standards for financial reporting. Although Snippet 2 doesn't explicitly state the timeframe or certification requirements, the Perturbation Explanation suggests that the original contract's stipulations ensured compliance with timely and audited financial statements, aligning with potential regulations like the Securities Exchange Act. Thus, the modifications introduce a contradiction by potentially undermining compliance with financial reporting standards.

perturbation.original_text: If the Principal is a natural person, upon the death or mental incapacity of a Principal, the executor, administrator, or personal representative of such Principal shall transfer the Principal's interest in Franchisee to a third party approved by BKC within a reasonable time after the Principal's death or mental incapacity.
perturbation.changed_text: If the Principal is a natural person, upon the death or mental incapacity of a Principal, the executor, administrator, or personal representative of such Principal shall transfer the Principal's interest in Franchisee to a third party approved by BKC within five (5) years after the Principal's death or mental incapacity.
perturbation.explanation: Extending the timeframe for transferring the interest to 5 years could potentially violate estate tax laws or regulations regarding the timely settlement and distribution of assets.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 26 U.S. Code § 6075 - Time for filing estate tax returns
perturbation.law_explanation: 26 U.S. Code § 6075 specifies the timeframe for filing estate tax returns, which are closely linked to the settlement of an estate and the distribution of its assets. A 5-year delay may not align with the requirements for efficient tax calculation and reporting.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 26 U.S. Code § 6075 - Time for filing estate and gift tax returns | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 26 U.S. Code § 6075 - Time for filing estate and gift tax returns U.S. Code Notes prev | next (a) Estate tax returns Returns made under section 6018(a) (relating to estate taxes) shall be filed within 9 months after the date of the decedent’s death. (b) Gift tax returns (1) General rule Returns made under section 6019 (relating to gift taxes) shall be filed on or before the 15th day of April following the close of the calendar year. (2) Extension where taxpayer granted extension for filing income tax return Any extension of time granted the taxpayer for filing the return of income taxes imposed by subtitle A for any taxable year which is a calendar year shall be deemed to be also an extension of time granted the taxpayer for filing the return under section 6019 for such calendar year. (3) Coordination with due date for estate tax return Notwithstanding paragraphs (1) and (2), the time for filing the return made under section 6019 for the calendar year which includes the date of death of the donor shall not be later than the time (including extensions) for filing the return made under section 6018 (relating to estate tax returns) with respect to such donor. perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces a specific timeframe of five years for transferring the Principal's interest, whereas the original text stipulated a 'reasonable time.' This modification contradicts 26 U.S. Code § 6075, which mandates that estate tax returns 'shall be filed within 9 months after the date of the decedent's death.' By extending the timeframe to five years, the changed text creates a scenario where the transfer of assets could potentially delay or complicate the estate tax filing process, thereby contradicting the legal requirement for timely filing as outlined in the law snippet.
